context.text: More, more. Basically a decimal is a number that's based on the number 10. Actually, all of our numbers are decimals because our number system is based on 10. Even the word decimal comes from the Latin word for 10, decum. When people talk about decimals, they're talking about numbers that are only parts of a whole. Imagine you've got a rectangle that represents one whole object. If you divide it into 10, smaller, equal- sized rectangles, you now have 10 parts of 1, tenths. If you divide each of those into 10 smaller rectangles, you then have 10 times 10 or 100 parts of 1, hundredths. Then if you divide those into 10 smaller rectangles, you have 10 times 100, or 1,000 parts of 1, thousandths. All of these fractions, tenths, hundredths, and thousandths, are called decimal units. Writing a decimal is pretty easy. In our number system, I can count from 0 to 9 in a place. Each place increases by the power of 10. Because decimal units are smaller than one unit, you actually move to the right of the decimal place. To do that, meet this guy, the decimal point. Numbers to the left of the decimal point are whole numbers, and numbers to the right are decimals. Except for the units, every place on the left side has a decimal equivalent on the right, tens and tenths, hundreds and hundredths, thousandths and thousandths. This number is thirty-nine and eighty-three hundredths. Actually, decimals are really important. We use them everyday to talk about money. Right, one cent is just one hundredth of a dollar. We also use decimals for weight, measuring distances, baseball statistics, temperatures, and even stock prices. Do you need more reasons? Well, arithmetic is a lot easier with decimals than it is with fractions. As long as you line the decimal points up, you can add and subtract them just like whole numbers. Also, decimal numbers are easier to compare. By adding zeros to the right of the shorter number to make the numbers the same length, you can tell right away which one is bigger. Zero is just a place holder. Adding zeros to the right of the decimal doesn't change the value of the number. Some numbers do go on adding zeros to the right forever. For example, the decimal equivalent of the fraction one third starts out as .333, and just keeps going on and on. These decimals are called repeating decimals. You could round that number off to three tenths, or thirty- three hundredths, but it's more accurate to put a line over the last number. That line indicates that the number keeps on going. Decimals that end are called terminating decimals, but some decimals, called irrational numbers, don't end or repeat. One example is pi, which is the ratio or circumference of circle to its diameter. Pi starts off 3.14159 – then it just keeps going for so long that scientists have found millions of its decimals without any repeating pattern. Convinced yet? One hundredth of a second. See? Decimals matter.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: When Sam is 40, how old will Chris be?

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: Interesting, walk us through your thinking—how did you use the numbers in the table to come up with 35?
alternatives.productiveFailure: If Sam is 35 when Chris is 30, let’s look back at the table—does that same relationship work for when Chris was 9 and Sam was 12?
alternatives.uptake: So if Sam is 35, what relationship are you seeing between Chris's age and Sam's age that we can check against the rest of the table?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: How did you arrive at 35; what pattern are you seeing in the table to get that number?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: That’s because the answer is not there. We took 8 and multiplied it by four fifths. We wanted to know what four fifths of 8 was, because this gave me thirty-two fifths.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Check out the glue. That fraction bar actually means division. This is saying 32 divided by 5. Turn it into a mixed number. How many times can 5 go into 32? What’s the closest it can get? 30. How many times is that?

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: Tell me more about what you mean by changing the denominators—how would that help us with thirty-two fifths?
alternatives.productiveFailure: Try that on your paper and see what happens. If you change the denominators, does that help you figure out how many whole gallons are in thirty-two fifths?
alternatives.uptake: You’re thinking about changing the denominators; what would that look like here, and how would it help us make sense of this thirty-two fifths?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: Can you explain how changing the denominators would help us solve this multiplication problem?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Nope, zero’s not a factor. Five’s one, and?

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: If we multiply six by another whole number, can we land exactly on twenty-five?
alternatives.productiveFailure: If you count by sixes, do you land exactly on twenty-five, or is there a remainder?
alternatives.uptake: If we count by sixes, do we land exactly on 25, or do we end up skipping over it?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: What would you multiply 6 by to get exactly 25?

context.speaker: student
context.text: Can we make our own shapes?

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Student A, have you been in class today? Why don’t you follow directions? Student S, you have to combine those shapes. You can’t just have one shape. You have to use two or more shapes. Yes. Okay. Student L, she distracting you two? You guys aren’t following directions. You have to use two or more shapes. Combine – pay attention. You have to combine the shapes to make a new shape. So for instance, I gave you an example on the board. I’m going to give you another one for a four sided shape. You have C and you have B, all right? That’s 1, 2, 3, 4. I just combined C and B to make a four sided shape. That’s what you have to do. Let me see if I have some. Remember you’re combining shapes. Love it. Bonus points for you. Yeah. But she just did it. Did you do it as well or did Student B do it? I think Student A did too. You can make a three sided shape using two or more polygons. You get bonus points. I don’t think I have enough there. Oh well, just do it on the back. It’s not a big deal. Here we go. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Where are you getting 7? Over there. Okay. Where are they? You have the polygons too. Okay. Count those sides. No. Start here. No, you don’t count the inside. Excuse me. Lower your voice. Do your work. They are listening right now and seeing you’re not paying attention. So do your work, please. How many sides?

context.speaker: student
context.text: I thought you just said to—

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Outside, 1, every time. Let me trace it. 1. Right? Every time you make a turn, think of the angle as a turn. You’re going around it. 2, 3. Now I’m just going to draw a dotted line here. 4. 5, right? It’s like the perimeter. You go around the outside and combine the shapes. This is K and L, and that would be a five sided shape. That’s a pentagon. It would go over – where is your other one? That would go right here, okay? All right. It’s a five sided shape. Let me see. Did you count this? Count this little piece right here. 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. Excellent job. You done anything girls? So 1, 2, 3. Now if you did this, you see how this is flush right here? This is going to be one side because your polygons are the same size there. That’s going to have no break in it. That’s going to be one side. That’s five. Okay. 1, 2, bring it down to your 4. Make that your four sided. Okay. Looks like a snow cone. I love this. I love it.

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: Tell me more about those three; which sides are you counting?
alternatives.productiveFailure: Trace the outside of your new shape with your finger—how many straight edges do you feel all the way around the perimeter?
alternatives.uptake: You found 3 sides so far; if you keep tracing all the way around the outside edge, how many more do we need to count to get back to where you started?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: How did you count those? Show me where you see those three sides.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Okay, so it’s going to be here, then.

context.speaker: teacher
context.text: Yeah, you got to break your number apart. Seventy. And then -

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: Tell me more about how you got eighty from the number two hundred seventy-three.
alternatives.productiveFailure: Try adding that eighty to the two hundred you already have and see if that matches the number we are trying to break apart.
alternatives.uptake: Eighty? If we use eighty there, how does that help us represent the seventy that is part of our two hundred seventy-three?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: How did you decide on eighty when we look at the value of the digits in two hundred seventy-three?

alternatives.dialogicTeaching: I hear both "two" and "one"—can you explain your thinking so we can see how you arrived at that?
alternatives.productiveFailure: If two goes into two twice, let's check our work by multiplying it back: what would two times two be?
alternatives.uptake: I hear both "two" and "one"—if we have two and we're seeing how many times two fits inside of it, how many groups of two can we actually make?
alternatives.pressingForReasoning: I’m hearing both one and two. For those of you who said two, how are you thinking about that? Walk us through your logic.
